items.question: When does Git produce a merge conflict?
options: when both sides changed the same lines (or one deleted what the other modified)
items.answer: when both sides changed the same lines (or one deleted what the other modified)
items.explanation: Non-overlapping changes auto-merge; overlapping ones can't be decided.

items.question: What do the conflict markers `<<<<<<< HEAD ... ======= ... >>>>>>> branch` represent?
options: the two sides Git couldn't reconcile, separated by =======
items.answer: the two sides Git couldn't reconcile, separated by =======
items.explanation: HEAD side above the `=======`; incoming-branch side below.

items.question: What's the right sequence after editing conflicted files?
items.explanation: Staging marks the file resolved; then continue the merge/rebase.

items.question: How do you bail out of an in-progress merge or rebase?
items.explanation: Returns to the pre-operation state with your working tree intact.

items.question: Why does `diff3` conflict style make resolution easier?
options: it shows the common ancestor between the two sides
items.answer: it shows the common ancestor between the two sides
items.explanation: Seeing the baseline reveals what each side changed *from* — usually decisive.

items.question: Notebook conflicts are best handled with:
items.explanation: nbdime gives cell-level semantic diff; nbstripout keeps outputs out of Git in the first place.
